free_text: Spallation and fission product yields for 232Th
bombarded with 140 MeV alphas
The University of Maryland Cyclotron.
The Ge(Li) detector used in this study
had a resolution of 2.0 keV full width at half-
maximum (FWHM) at 1332 keV and an active volume of
about 50 cm3. Five high resolution silicon
surface barrier detectors (~50 keV FWHM).
Both thin (1 mg/cm2) and thick (60 mg/ cm2) thorium
foils were irradiated.
After the irradiations, the foils were
generally placed about 30 cm from the front face of a
lithium-drifted germanium detector. Some of the foils
were permitted to decay for about one hour to allow
short-lived activities to become negligible. In other
cases, gamma-ray counting was initiated with only the
five minute delay required to transfer the irradiated
foil to the counting station. Gamma-ray spectra were
accumulated sequentially for up to several days by a
microcomputer-based multichannel analyzer and
recorded on magnetic tape. The counting sequences
were appropriately selected to permit half-life
identification over a range from 2.5 min to 30 d.
Several of the neutron-deficient nuclides decay
primarily by alpha-particle emission rather than
beta-decay. These products were measured with a
Geneva wheel mechanism in which six carbon catcher
foils are attached to a main disk at 60 degrees
angles. The carbon foils caught the recoiling
reaction products formed when the cyclotron beam
bombarded a 100 microg/cm2 thorium foil. The
alpha-decays of the spallation products captured by
the carbon foils were detected by five high
resolution silicon surface barrier detectors.
The gamma-ray spectra were analyzed with a
light-pen interactive version of the program PEAKFIND
which permitted the overlay off its and spectra on the
CRT display screen. Alpha spectra were analyzed by a
similar program ANALYSIS which permitted greater
flexibility in adjustment of peak-shape parameters
and allowed a greater number of peaks to be
simultaneously analyzed. Once the energies and peak
areas were measured for both the gamma-rays and
alpha-particles the half-lives of the peaks present
in the sequential spectra were determined to make
positive identification of the radioisotopes present.
Cross sections were calculated by using the
appropriate corrections.
The growth and decay corrections for each product and
correcting for change in detector dead time during the
sequential counting. In addition to providing a
method of distinguishing between spallation and
fission product activities, the use of two foil
thicknesses also facilitated a method of correcting
for recoiling fission and spallation products which
escape from near the foil surface. Corrections for
losses of fission products relied on using the ranges
of fission products in uranium metal.

The limits of error for measured cross
sections were estimated on the basis of the errors in
fitted peak areas, errors in detector efficiency,
and how well the decay curves fit the known half-lives
of the isotopes.
